schools.summary: Gill lays out the whole array of proposed solutions to the twenty-seventh-year discrepancy without committing to one.
Ellicott cuts the knot: 'twenty-seventh' is an error of transcription for the fifteenth year, fixed by the figures of chapter 14.
Clarke despairs of the chronology ('probably some mistake in the numbers') and, with Kennicott, corrects the king's name to Uzziah; Wesley reads 'to reign' as reigning solely and fully.

debates.question: How is 'the twenty-seventh year of Jeroboam' to be reconciled with the figures of the previous chapters?

sides.position: a textual error of transcription for the fifteenth year
a co-regency or eleven-year interregnum during Uzziah's minority, the throne held by protectors
the years reckoned forward to the extinction of Jehu's house
holders: Ben Gersom and Abarbanel (reported by Gill)

sides.position: the numbers are simply corrupt and past sure recovery

themes: Azariah in the inscriptions of Tiglath-Pileser

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch explain the formula's silence: Uzziah's great works (2 Chron. 26) are omitted because Ahaz undid them, and his godliness lasted only while the prophet Zechariah lived.
Gill notes the peaceable succession by consent of the people and princes of Judah.

themes: piety tethered to a living prophet

schools.summary: Ellicott defines 'did right' as support of the legitimate worship and no foreign cultus, reconciling the estimate with the Chronicler's 'as long as he sought Jehovah.'
Gill qualifies the commendation as true only 'at the beginning' and 'in an external way.'
Clarke catalogues from 2 Chron. 26:5 the wars, buildings, wells, and army that were Uzziah's reward while he sought God.
Gill sets the surviving high places in the long pattern of Judah's good kings who all left them standing until Hezekiah.

themes: the chronic exception to a good reign

schools.summary: Clarke tells the full 2 Chron. 26:16 scene of the usurped censer and adds Charles Wesley's verses on 'the leprosy of pride'; Wesley points back to the same chapter for the cause.
Ellicott parses 'several house' as a sickhouse or 'house of freedom' and reads Jotham as viceroy, not co-regent.
Gill gives the reason in a line: the king intruded into the priest's office to burn incense.

outliers.note: Turns the leprosy-regency into an argument against the joint-sovereignty device so often used to fix the chapter's chronology: Jotham is viceroy of a diseased father, not a co-regent sharing the crown.

themes: leprosy as judgment on royal pride

schools.summary: Ellicott inventories Uzziah's deferred acts — wars, army, husbandry, and the fatal intrusion into the sanctuary — directing the reader to 2 Chronicles 26.
Gill notes some acts survive in Chronicles and others were written by the prophet Isaiah.
Keil and Delitzsch note from the Chronicler that the leprous king was buried near, not in, the royal tomb — 'in the burial-field of the kings.'
Gill marks the plain death of the king after fifty-two years.

Keil and Delitzsch argue for an eleven-year interregnum after Jeroboam (favoured by the swift struggle and Hosea's allusions); Ellicott instead posits no interregnum, Jeroboam having reigned fifty-one years.
Henry surveys the whole thirty-three-year anarchy — murder after murder, the promise to Jehu fulfilled, the calves never forsaken, Assyria preying on a divided land.
Gill reckons an interregnum 'of twenty-two years at least,' owing to dissension and dislike of Zachariah.
Wesley notes the six-month reign came after the throne stood vacant several years through the kingdom's dissensions.

debates.question: How long was the interregnum between Jeroboam II and Zachariah?

sides.position: none at all — Jeroboam reigned fifty-one years

themes: the collapse of the northern kingdom

schools.summary: Ellicott identifies 'his fathers' as the dynasty of Jehu; Keil and Delitzsch read the murder as the judgment of rejection on a nation that spurned every mercy and warning.
Gill glosses the evil as the calf-worship of Jeroboam, never departed from.
Ellicott reads the open assassination 'before the people' as a symptom of moral corruption and 'son of Jabesh' as a usurper's patronymic; Keil carries the story to Menahem's counter-stroke.
Gill notes Shallum was a friend of the king, emboldened by popular discontent.
Clarke reads 'before the people' as a public assembly, the king having grown odious.

outliers.note: Conjectures that 'Qobol-am' ('before the people') is really the proper name of another usurper, comparing the 'third king' of Zech. 11:8 — a reading Ellicott judges incoherent.
Would emend the difficult word to 'in Ibleam,' a place-name.

schools.summary: Gill notes the formula must cover not only the six-month reign but Zachariah's acts during the long interregnum before it.

themes: the standing formula for a doomed king
the dignity of form over substance
the last of Jehu's house dismissed in a line

schools.summary: Clarke counts the four generations (Jehoahaz, Joash, Jeroboam, Zachariah) and pairs the kept promise with the judgment on Jehu's cruelty foretold in Hosea 1:4.
Gill notes the word to Jehu was now fulfilled in Zachariah's short reign.
Ellicott reports Thenius's view that the reflection was added by the Judean editor.

outliers.note: Regards the prophecy-fulfilment remark as a later Judean editor's addition to the abstract of Zachariah's reign.

themes: the promise exhausted at the fourth generation

schools.summary: Wesley draws the moral that dominion founded in blood seldom lasts; Clarke narrates Menahem's march from Tirzah, the slaying of Shallum, and the assault on Tiphsah.
Gill fixes the date and the interchangeable names Uzziah/Azariah.
Ellicott renders 'a full month' literally and records Thenius's objection from Zech. 11:8.

debates.question: Did Shallum reign a literal full month?

sides.position: yes, a full month as the Hebrew says
no — the three kings of Zech. 11:8 must be crowded into one month

schools.summary: Ellicott names Menahem in Tiglath-Pileser's tribute-list ('Menihimmè Samerinâ'a', c. 738 B.C.) and reconstructs the march on Samaria; Keil carries the account to the ten-year reign and the sack of Tiphsah.
Gill locates Tirzah, the old royal city in Manasseh six miles from Samaria.

themes: the avenger who becomes the usurper

schools.summary: Gill observes that Shallum's memorial is his 'conspiracy which he made' — the annals would have named its cause and accomplices.

themes: a king remembered by his treason
the conspiracy as the sum of the man
the formula bent to include a crime
an epitaph on the anarchy of the age

schools.summary: Ellicott places Tiphsah near Tirzah, reconstructs Menahem's terror-campaign to compel submission, and notes Thenius's emendation to Tappuah.
Gill rejects the Euphrates Thapsacus for a town near Samaria and Tirzah, about six miles off.

debates.question: Where was the Tiphsah that Menahem smote?

sides.position: the famous Thapsacus on the Euphrates
a local Israelite town near Tirzah

themes: ripping up the women with child

schools.summary: Ellicott notes the round 'ten years' carried 'some months over,' the formula's totals being approximations.
Gill observes that Shallum's one month and Menahem's reign both fall in the same thirty-ninth year of Azariah.

themes: the first settled reign since Jeroboam II

schools.summary: Ellicott argues 'all his days' is misplaced and belongs to the next verse, and prefers the LXX 'from all the sins'; Keil questions the tradition that Menahem invited Pul.
Gill notes the same character is given Menahem as of those before — the calf-worship unforsaken.

outliers.note: Holds that Menahem appeared guided by better principles at the outset, on the strength of the shepherd-imagery of Zech. 11:4-8.

themes: was the Assyrian summoned or endured

schools.summary: Ellicott lays out Schrader's ten-step argument that Pul and Tiglath-Pileser are one person, reads 'came upon and occupied,' and finds Menahem's invitation in the very phrase.
Gill insists Pul was an Assyrian, the first the Scriptures name, distinct from the Babylonian Belesis.
Clarke marks the first mention of Assyria since Nimrod (Gen. 10:11) and, with Prideaux, makes Pul the father of Sardanapalus.

debates.question: Who was Pul, and was he summoned by Menahem?

sides.position: a distinct earlier Assyrian king, father of Sardanapalus, reigning forty-eight years
Tiglath-Pileser himself under an alternate name, invited by Menahem to make him a vassal

themes: a throne propped up with tribute
vassalage to the empire that will destroy Israel

schools.summary: Ellicott computes the exaction — a thousand talents at fifty shekels a head implies sixty thousand men of property, there being no Temple treasury in the north; Keil marks Pul as the first Assyrian to invade Israel.
Gill notes Menahem laid the tax on the wealthy and so spared the poor.
Clarke reckons the assessment at upwards of five pounds sterling a man.

themes: the tribute wrung from the wealthy
the first Assyrian footfall on Israel

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch carry the formula to the succession — Menahem dies in peace, and his son Pekahiah reigns two years before his aide-de-camp Pekah kills him.
Gill calls the formula 'a form the historian uses of them all.'
Clarke notes bleakly that no chronicles survive with anything further about this king.
Gill marks the paradox that Menahem, an usurper and tyrant, 'died a natural death, and in peace.'

themes: judgment deferred to the next generation
a quiet death is no acquittal

schools.summary: Gill accounts for a one-year interregnum before Pekahiah, whose disputed title kept him a year from the throne.
Keil and Delitzsch narrate the palace assassination by Pekah with Argob, Arieh, and fifty Gileadites, and flag the twenty-year reign as a chronological knot.
Gill calls the calf-worship 'a piece of state policy all the kings of Israel gave into.'
Gill parses Pekah's title as a 'third man' or, with Josephus, a chiliarch — a trusted officer whose treachery struck from within.
Clarke reports the divided opinion on Argob and Arieh and the detail of Pekah's fifty Gileadites.

debates.question: Were Argob and Arieh men or statues?

sides.position: men — fellow-conspirators or courtiers slain with the king
statues or palace fixtures near which the murder was done

themes: a murder recorded half in shadow

schools.summary: Gill notes only that 'the same form of expression is used as before, of all the kings.'
Gill fixes Pekah's accession in Azariah's fifty-second and last year, binding the two kingdoms' transitions together.

themes: reprieve in Judah, ruin in Israel

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch set the formula beside the Assyrian deportation and the tangled 'twentieth year of Jotham,' resolved (with Usher) by the mode of counting.
Gill spreads the charge over Pekah's treason, murder, and calf-worship.
Clarke traces the long history of the lost cities and adds from 1 Chron. 5:26 that the trans-Jordan tribes were carried off and 'never restored.'
Gill dwells on the forms and etymology of Tiglath-Pileser's name and his link to the Pul of v.19.
Wesley equates 'the twentieth year of Jotham' with the fourth of Ahaz; Clarke, following Calmet, stages Hoshea's rise from conspiracy to partial to full possession (2 Kings 17:1).
Gill catches the 'measure for measure' — the slayer of his king slain in turn.

debates.question: How can Hoshea's coup fall 'in the twentieth year of Jotham' when Jotham reigned only sixteen?

sides.position: count twenty years from Jotham's accession, i.e. the fourth year of Ahaz
a staged seizure — conspiracy in Pekah's twentieth year, mastery of part in Ahaz's fourth, full control in Ahaz's twelfth

themes: the last coup of the chapter
dating past Jotham's death into Ahaz

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch use the seam to check the arithmetic: Jotham's accession in Pekah's second year agrees with v.27, his sixteen years ending in Pekah's seventeenth (2 Kings 16:1).
Gill notes the standing deferral to the book of chronicles.
Henry gives the whole warm portrait of Jotham — pious, just, builder of the higher gate, dead at forty-one, in whose reign the Syro-Ephraimite storm first gathered against his degenerate son.
Gill works the synchronism, Jotham succeeding at the beginning of Pekah's second year.

themes: recorded ages only for Judah's kings
beautifying the temple he could not purify
the gathering confederacy of Rezin and Pekah

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch frame the reign like Uzziah's and note that Rezin and Pekah's raids began already in Jotham's closing years.
Gill clarifies that the sixteen years are Jotham's sole reign, after the regency of the leprosy years.

themes: the recorded lineage of David's house
the first raids of the confederacy

schools.summary: Gill gathers Jarchi's 'nothing scandalous' and Josephus's high character, grounding the commendation in Jotham's freedom from idolatry.

themes: right defined as fidelity in worship

schools.summary: Gill sets the surviving high places in the standing pattern of the best kings before Hezekiah.
Wesley locates the higher gate not in the temple but in one of its courts, probably toward the king's palace.
Clarke unfolds from 2 Chronicles 27 Jotham's buildings, cities, towers, and victory over Ammon, and weighs whether the higher gate is Jeremiah's 'new gate.'
Gill points to Chronicles for Jotham's other acts.

themes: the higher gate and the 'new gate'

schools.summary: Clarke sets these first raids at the head of the ruinous Assyrian wars, with a pastor's compassion and a guarded hope for the Jews' future.
Gill fixes 'in those days' as the end of Jotham's reign, the threat forming under him but falling on Ahaz.

themes: men as the rod of God
the storm gathered under a pious king
the threshold of the Assyrian ruin

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch simply carry the burial forward to the accession of Ahaz.
Gill notes Jotham buried with the kings of Judah in their sepulchres.
